title: openclaw-plugin: comprehensive client + config + gateway tests
sec: restrict bypassToolNames to hardcoded plugin-only allowlist
body: CRITICAL (pentest-governance finding 1): bypassToolNames is read wholesale from external config with no validation. An attacker or malicious openclaw.json can list any tool to bypass governance completely, silently, with no audit trail. Fix: validate bypassToolNames against a hardcoded allowlist of Portarium introspection tools only (portarium_get_run, portarium_list_approvals, portarium_capability_lookup). Reject or strip any other values. Log every bypass event. Files: packages/openclaw-plugin/src/config.ts, hooks/before-tool-call.ts

title: sec: authenticate /metrics endpoint — currently unauthenticated
body: CRITICAL (pentest-api finding 1, pentest-governance finding 4): GET /metrics is registered before auth middleware with no bearer-token check. Leaks workspaceId, approval throughput, action execution rates to unauthenticated callers. Fix: move metrics to the existing health-check server (separate port) or add a bearer-token/IP-allowlist check. File: src/presentation/runtime/control-plane-handler.ts:464

title: fix: plugin config numeric validation — floor/isFinite guards for approvalTimeoutMs and pollIntervalMs
body: CRITICAL/HIGH (bug-hunter 1,2,9; pentest-governance 6): approvalTimeoutMs=0 silently makes the poller never run (instant deny). pollIntervalMs=0 creates a CPU spin-loop making thousands of HTTP calls/sec. NaN/Infinity pass typeof===number check. Fix: use Number.isFinite(x) && x > 0 guard in resolveConfig. Minimum floor: approvalTimeoutMs >= 60000, pollIntervalMs >= 500. File: packages/openclaw-plugin/src/config.ts

title: sec: CRLF injection via workspaceId/tenantId/bearerToken in HTTP headers
body: HIGH (bug-hunter finding 3): workspaceId, tenantId, and bearerToken are used as raw HTTP header values with no CRLF validation. A value containing \r\n enables HTTP header injection. resolveConfig only checks non-empty string. Fix: strip or reject CRLF (\r, \n, \0) from these three values in resolveConfig. Also sanitize sessionKey (agentId) — agent-controlled, no length limit or char validation (pentest-governance finding 7). Files: packages/openclaw-plugin/src/config.ts, hooks/before-tool-call.ts

title: sec: change example failClosed default to true + add production hardening notes
body: HIGH (pentest-governance finding 2): examples/openclaw/workspace-config.json ships with failClosed: false. An attacker who disrupts the control plane gets a fully ungated agent. Fix: change failClosed to true in workspace-config.json and setup.md. Add prominent warning that failClosed:false is for local dev only. Consider startup assertion in plugin rejecting failClosed:false when NODE_ENV is not development. Files: examples/openclaw/workspace-config.json, examples/openclaw/setup.md, packages/openclaw-plugin/src/config.ts

title: fix: poller silent error path — add logging, backoff, and Executed status handling
body: MEDIUM (bug-hunter 4,5): (1) Poller error path has no logging — 28800 failed HTTP calls over 24h with zero operator visibility. Inject logger, log on first error and every Nth. Add max-consecutive-error threshold to exit early. (2) Executed status (added bead-0928) not in parseApprovalStatus — falls through to error, poller keeps going for 24h. Fix: add 'executed' and any other terminal statuses. Files: packages/openclaw-plugin/src/services/approval-poller.ts, packages/openclaw-plugin/src/client/portarium-client.ts

title: fix: content-type validation before JSON parse in plugin client
body: LOW (pentest-governance finding 10): portarium-client calls response.json() without checking content-type. A MITM returning HTML 200 throws SyntaxError caught as status:error — with failClosed:false this bypasses governance. Fix: verify content-type starts with application/json before calling .json(). Return status:error if not. File: packages/openclaw-plugin/src/client/portarium-client.ts:101

title: fix: experiment scripts — null guard on approvalId + conditional operator-approve
body: MEDIUM (bug-hunter 7,8): (1) All experiments use approvalId without null guard — if propose returns non-202, GET /approvals/undefined proceeds giving misleading results. Add bail-out: if (!approvalId) { record FAILED and return early }. (2) Operator-approve step runs unconditionally after self-approve check — if self-approve returned 200 (governance failure), experiment records confusingly mixed outcome. Guard operator-approve with: if (selfApprove.status === 403). Files: examples/openclaw/run-timed-experiments.mjs, examples/openclaw/business-scenarios/run-business-experiments.mjs

title: fix: plugin health-check uses bare fetch instead of injected fetchImpl
body: LOW-MED (bug-hunter finding 6): The portarium.status health-check in index.ts:105 calls globalThis.fetch directly instead of the injected this.#fetchImpl used by all other client methods. Prevents test mocking and diverges from polyfilled environments. Fix: expose a ping() method on PortariumClient using #fetchImpl, or pass client into the gateway handler. File: packages/openclaw-plugin/src/index.ts:105

title: sec: IDOR on GET /approvals/:id — add app-layer ownership check + fix in-memory store
body: HIGH (pentest-api finding 2): In-memory bootstrap store ignores tenantId and workspaceId in getApprovalById — any workspace can read any approval. App layer has no post-fetch ownership assertion so any future store regression creates real IDOR. Fix: (1) Fix in-memory store to filter by workspaceId. (2) Add explicit ownership check in get-approval.ts after fetch: if (approval.workspaceId !== input.workspaceId) return err({kind:Forbidden}). Files: src/application/queries/get-approval.ts, in-memory bootstrap store

title: sec: approval→execution maker-checker gap — operator can execute any approved action
body: HIGH (pentest-api finding 3): operator role has agent-action:execute permission but not approval:submit. The execute path only checks approval.status===Approved, not who approved it. An operator can execute an action approved by themselves (via admin hat). Fix: add maker-checker constraint in execute-approved-agent-action.ts: executing userId cannot equal approval.decidedByUserId. Files: src/application/iam/rbac/workspace-rbac.ts, src/application/commands/execute-approved-agent-action.ts

title: fix: validate status query param at HTTP layer for list-approvals
body: HIGH (pentest-api finding 4): status query param is cast directly to ApprovalStatus without HTTP-layer enum validation. limit param passes NaN/-1 to app layer. Fix: validate status against APPROVAL_STATUSES allowlist at the HTTP handler before passing to use-case. Validate limit is a positive integer. File: src/presentation/runtime/control-plane-handler.approvals.ts:173

title: sec: sanitize error messages in 500/502 responses — no raw Error.message in detail field
body: MEDIUM (pentest-api finding 5): Raw Error.message from infrastructure exceptions (DB errors, downstream failures) leak into RFC 9457 detail fields. Can expose PostgreSQL schema, table names, internal host/port, file paths. Fix: use generic message for unexpected/infra errors in all error handlers. Log actual error server-side with correlationId. Files: src/presentation/runtime/control-plane-handler.ts:953, control-plane-handler.agent-action-execute.ts:60,68, control-plane-handler.approvals.ts:69,77

title: sec: require JWT issuer + audience at startup when JWKS_URI is configured
body: MEDIUM (pentest-api finding 6, pentest-governance finding 9): PORTARIUM_JWT_ISSUER and PORTARIUM_JWT_AUDIENCE are optional — server starts with warnings but accepts tokens from any issuer. A staging env without these vars accepts tokens from any IdP. Fix: when PORTARIUM_JWKS_URI is set, fail-fast at startup if PORTARIUM_JWT_ISSUER or PORTARIUM_JWT_AUDIENCE are absent. Add to checkStoreBootstrapGate pattern. File: src/presentation/runtime/control-plane-handler.bootstrap.ts:74

title: sec: orphan approvals from idempotency race — submit-approval must verify linked proposal exists
body: HIGH (pentest-governance finding 5): propose-agent-action documents an accepted race condition where the losing concurrent request creates an orphan ApprovalPendingV1 record. submit-approval.ts does NOT verify the approval's linked proposalId matches a winning proposal. A human operator can approve the orphan — decision recorded, no tool call unblocked, audit trail has phantom entry. High-concurrency flood creates operator queue DoS. Fix: submit-approval must fetch the linked proposal and reject decisions on orphaned approvals. Or wrap propose+save in a single DB transaction. Files: src/application/commands/propose-agent-action.ts:220, src/application/commands/submit-approval.ts

title: fix: rate limiting bypassed for requests with empty workspace segment
body: LOW (pentest-api finding 7): Rate limiting middleware at /v1/workspaces/* skips rate check when workspace segment is empty (regex returns null, rawId is empty string, falls through to next()). Fix: apply a global rate limit by IP or authenticated tenant BEFORE the workspace-specific limiter, as a catch-all for requests that don't match the workspace pattern. File: src/presentation/runtime/control-plane-handler.ts:500,508

Epic: Autonomous B2B Growth Studio — live experiment showcase
body:
## Goal
Demonstrate OpenClaw + Portarium end-to-end in a real business scenario:
an autonomous B2B growth studio that researches prospects, plans outreach,
creates content, requests human approval via Cockpit triage cards, executes
approved actions, and measures results — looping continuously.

## Business loop
research → plan → create assets → request approval → execute → measure → iterate

## Business models combined
1. Autonomous B2B growth studio (LinkedIn outreach, cold email, CRM)
2. Autonomous niche media engine (blog posts, social content, newsletters)

## Success criteria
- Full loop runs live via OpenRouter LLM with real tool calls
- Every mutation goes through Portarium approval (HumanApprove tier)
- Cockpit triage cards show rich context for operator decisions
- Evidence chain captures full audit trail (hash-linked)
- Metrics dashboard shows funnel: proposed → approved → executed → measured
- Demo can be recorded as a compelling 3-minute video

title: Epic: Autonomous B2B Growth Studio — live experiment suite

- Each persona's goal, tools, autonomy tier, and approval requirements

- Input/output contracts between stages

## Acceptance
- Spec follows existing .specify format

- Approval checkpoints explicitly listed per stage

title: Experiment 1: Prospect Research Agent — auto-research via ReadOnly tools
Tool registry: define Growth Studio tools + classification tiers
body:
## What
Define the tool set for the Growth Studio agents and classify each by risk tier:

### ReadOnly (Auto tier)
- web-search: search Google/Bing for prospect info
- scrape-website: extract text from a public URL
- read-crm-contact: fetch contact record from CRM
- read-analytics: fetch campaign metrics

### Mutation (HumanApprove tier)
- draft-email: compose outreach email (saved as draft, not sent)
- draft-linkedin-post: compose LinkedIn post draft
- draft-blog-article: generate blog article draft
- update-crm-contact: update CRM contact fields/notes
- schedule-content: add content to publishing queue

### Dangerous (ManualOnly tier)
- send-email: actually send an email to external recipient
- publish-linkedin-post: publish to LinkedIn
- publish-blog-article: publish article to live site
- delete-crm-contact: remove a contact record

## Acceptance
- Each tool has: name, description, inputSchema (TypeBox), classification tier
- Tools registered in openclaw-plugin tool-classification patterns
- Unit test verifying tier assignment for each tool

title: Experiment 2: Outreach Draft Agent — create email + LinkedIn message, require approval
Approval policy: Growth Studio policy rules + SoD constraints
body:
## What
Define Portarium approval policies for the Growth Studio experiment:

### Policy rules
1. All ReadOnly tools → Auto (no approval needed)
2. draft-* tools → HumanApprove with 15min timeout, auto-deny on expiry
3. publish-*/send-* tools → ManualOnly (require explicit operator action)
4. Batch operations (>5 contacts) → escalate to ManualOnly regardless of tool tier
5. Budget guard: if estimated cost > $50 → escalate to ManualOnly

### SoD constraints
- MakerChecker: agent that proposes cannot self-approve
- DistinctApprovers: publish actions need different approver than draft approval
- Content review: draft approval must happen before publish approval

### Timeout behavior
- HumanApprove: 15 min timeout → auto-deny + notify operator
- ManualOnly: 60 min timeout → auto-deny + escalate to admin

## Acceptance
- Policies expressible in existing DSL condition format
- SoD constraints mapped to existing constraint types
- Integration test: policy evaluation returns correct tier for each tool

title: Orchestrator: Growth Studio pipeline coordinator script
body:
## What
Implement scripts/integration/growth-studio/orchestrator.ts — the top-level
coordinator that chains the 3 agent loops into a single pipeline run:

### Flow
1. Load ICP definition from config (JSON file or CLI arg)
2. Run Researcher agent → get ScoredProspect[]
3. Filter prospects by score threshold
4. For each qualifying prospect:
   a. Run ContentCreator agent → get ApprovedContent[]
   b. Run OutreachExecutor agent → get ExecutionResult[]
5. Aggregate results into PipelineReport
6. Log full evidence chain to file
7. Output metrics summary to stdout

### CLI interface
```
npx tsx scripts/integration/growth-studio/orchestrator.ts   --icp ./config/sample-icp.json   --dry-run          # skip actual sends, log what would happen
  --max-prospects 5  # cap research phase
```

## Acceptance
- Orchestrator chains all 3 agents in correct order
- Errors in one stage don't crash the pipeline (graceful per-prospect error handling)
- --dry-run mode works without OpenRouter key
- Pipeline report includes timing, counts, and evidence hashes
- Works with both live Portarium server and MSW mocks

title: E2E test: full Growth Studio loop — research → approve → execute → measure
body:
## What
Integration test that runs the full Growth Studio pipeline end-to-end:
- Uses MSW-intercepted Portarium API (no live server needed)
- Auto-approves HumanApprove requests after 1s delay (simulated operator)
- Auto-approves ManualOnly requests after 2s delay
- Verifies complete evidence chain from research through execution

### Test scenarios
1. Happy path: 1 prospect → 1 email draft → approved → sent → CRM updated
2. Denial path: draft denied → agent revises → re-approved → sent
3. Timeout path: approval expires → agent logs timeout, moves to next prospect
4. Batch escalation: >5 contacts → tier escalated to ManualOnly
5. SoD violation: same operator tries to approve draft + publish → blocked

## Acceptance
- All 5 scenarios pass in CI (npm run test)
- Evidence chain hash validation passes for each scenario
- Test runs in <30s (no real network, no real LLM calls)
- Coverage: orchestrator.ts, all 3 agent loops, policy evaluation

body:

### Metrics collected
- Per-prospect: outreach sent (bool), response received (bool), response sentiment

### Aggregation

title: Media engine: blog + newsletter + social content generation pipeline

## What

title: Gate: Growth Studio evidence review before prompt-language follow-up

Use the existing Growth Studio experiment as the hard gate before any prompt-language integration work starts.

- operator workflow quality: approval latency, triage clarity, denial and changes-requested loops

- whether the current OpenClaw plus Portarium stack already covers the use-case without prompt-language

## Acceptance
- Written go or no-go decision captured in the bead body or a linked note
- Explicit outcome: proceed, defer, or reject prompt-language follow-up

title: Spike: prompt-language architecture fit vs Lobster, OpenClaw, and Portarium
body:
## What
Compare prompt-language against the existing OpenClaw and Portarium execution surfaces before any build work starts.

Questions to answer:
- What belongs in prompt-language versus Lobster versus a native OpenClaw plugin?
- Should prompt-language stay an external runtime, become an OpenClaw-launched worker, or stay out of VAOP entirely?
- Where does the integration seam live without breaking Portarium architecture boundaries?
- How do we preserve fail-closed behaviour and avoid creating a governance bypass?

## Acceptance
- Clear recommendation: external runtime, wrapper integration, or no-go
- Responsibilities mapped across prompt-language, OpenClaw, and Portarium
- Proposed file and package location for any future work
- Risks, benefits, and non-goals recorded explicitly

title: Governance: Execution Tier and approval model for prompt-language coding workflows
body:
## What
Define the governance model required if prompt-language is ever used for governed coding workflows.

Cover:
- Execution Tier mapping for read-only repo work, code writes, test runs, git operations, external API writes, publishing, and deployment
- approval checkpoints and Separation of Duties constraints
- fail-closed rules when Portarium or OpenClaw governance is unavailable
- Evidence Artifact requirements for prompts, commands, outputs, and completion gates

## Acceptance
- Policy matrix mapping coding actions to Auto, HumanApprove, or ManualOnly
- No path allows prompt-language to bypass Portarium approvals for risky actions
- Operator review expectations and audit requirements are explicit
- Scope is reusable for future coding Workflow / Runbook experiments

title: Spike: governed coding Workflow / Runbook proof of concept using prompt-language
body:
## What
Build a thin proof of concept only if the evidence and architecture beads support moving forward.

Shape:
- use prompt-language as an external runtime for a small governed coding Workflow / Runbook
- keep it outside the production OpenClaw plugin path initially
- use a small demo target such as a tiny app scaffold, bugfix loop, or test-and-repair flow
- capture how completion gates, retries, and parallel work behave in practice

## Acceptance
- One repeatable proof-of-concept flow exists under an experimental or lab path
- The run demonstrates real completion gates, not prompt-only self-reporting
- Outputs and failure modes are captured for review
- Findings feed back into the architecture and governance decision

title: Integration: minimal governed prompt-language launch path after experiment validation
body:
## What
If the earlier beads conclude that prompt-language is a fit, add a minimal integration seam for governed use.

Possible shape:
- approved OpenClaw or Portarium launch of a prompt-language flow
- typed inputs and outputs for the coding run
- auditable handoff into Portarium Evidence Artifacts and approval history
- strict prevention of destructive or external actions outside approved policy

## Acceptance
- Integration point is intentionally chosen, not opportunistic
- The design preserves Portarium as the control plane and OpenClaw as the runtime host where applicable
- prompt-language remains a workflow-shaping layer, not a governance bypass
- Demo or smoke evidence proves the chosen seam works

title: Epic: prompt-language exploration for future governed coding workflows

No prompt-language integration work should start before the Growth Studio evidence gate is closed and the governance model is defined.
