- demo-machine is present in the local repo set
- it was not runnable on this workstation during the experiment slice
- the post-validation demo generation path remains unproven

Acceptance:
- demo-machine has a reproducible local preflight and runnable path for this workstation class, or
- the experiment toolchain marks it as intentionally unavailable with explicit skip semantics and rationale
- experiment reports can distinguish runnable vs intentionally skipped tools

title: bug: ci:pr dependency-cruiser script points at a missing entrypoint
body:
Observed repeatedly while validating the experiment work.

Problem:
- npm run ci:pr passes gates, migrations, typecheck, lint, format, and spell
- it then fails at depcruise because package.json points at node_modules/dependency-cruiser/bin/dependency-cruise.mjs
- that entrypoint is not present in the installed dependency-cruiser package version

Acceptance:
- depcruise and depgraph scripts use the correct installed dependency-cruiser entrypoint
- npm run depcruise succeeds locally
- npm run ci:pr can proceed past the architecture gate without this tooling failure

title: Epic: Iteration 2 governed business-scale experiment suite
body:
## Goal
Run the second wave of governed business-scale experiments without overwriting iteration 1.

## Intent
Iteration 1 proved that Portarium can govern live OpenClaw loops. Iteration 2 should test the
behaviour that matters in real businesses:
- overnight approvals and delayed resume
- operator-team handoff and out-of-order decisions
- multiple simultaneous governed sessions
- queue backlog, escalation, and expiry under sustained pressure
- stronger toolchain realism for content-machine and demo-machine

## Rule
- keep iteration 1 result bundles immutable
- create versioned experiment directories for reruns and second attempts
- capture timing, queue, resume, and evidence completeness metrics in every run

title: Observability: iteration 2 queue, resume, and backlog telemetry pack
body:
## What
Add the shared observability and evidence layer required by the iteration 2 experiment suite.

## Metrics to capture
- approval count by tier and by session
- pending age p50, p95, and max
- resume latency after approval decision
- blocked duration per agent session
- queue depth over time
- denial, request-changes, escalation, and expiry counts
- duplicate execution count
- evidence completeness count
- restart count and successful resume count

## Acceptance
- a shared experiment helper or telemetry pack can be reused by all iteration 2 experiment runners
- each experiment writes a durable timing and queue evidence artifact
- pass or fail conclusions can reference explicit thresholds rather than narrative only
- iteration 2 reports can compare runs on the same metric names

title: Experiment: growth-studio-openclaw-live-v2 overnight approval and exact resume
body:
## What
Create `growth-studio-openclaw-live-v2` as a rerun of the first live Growth Studio experiment with
business-length waiting behavior.

## Scenario
- OpenClaw proposes multiple governed Actions
- at least one Approval Gate is intentionally left pending for a long delay window
- the operator approves after the delay
- the agent must resume the blocked step exactly once without replaying prior writes

## Variants
- live-wait: the agent process remains alive while blocked
- restart-resume: the proposal survives process exit and a later recovery path resumes it

## Acceptance
- the pending approval remains durable across the full delay window
- resume latency after approval is measured and recorded
- no duplicate execution occurs after approval
- the Evidence Artifact chain is continuous before wait, during wait, and after resume
- the v2 report explicitly compares the result to the original growth-studio-openclaw-live run

title: Experiment: micro-saas-agent-stack-v2 operator team handoff
body:
## What
Create `micro-saas-agent-stack-v2` as an operator-team handoff experiment.

## Scenario
- one governed micro-SaaS Run creates multiple pending approvals
- Operator A handles low-risk draft approvals
- Operator B handles higher-risk publish or send decisions
- at least one item is denied or request-changes while other queued work proceeds

## Acceptance
- Separation of Duties is preserved when different approvers are required
- one denied or stalled approval does not corrupt unrelated queued work
- queue metrics show approver identity, wait time, and handoff timing
- result artifacts include a queue-state snapshot before and after operator actions
- the v2 report explicitly compares the result to the original micro-saas-agent-stack run

title: Experiment: openclaw-concurrent-sessions governed concurrency
body:
## What
Create `openclaw-concurrent-sessions` to test several governed OpenClaw sessions at once.

## Scenario
- launch multiple OpenClaw sessions concurrently
- each session proposes, waits, resumes, and writes to its own output bundle
- approvals are resolved in mixed order across the active sessions

## Acceptance
- approval IDs, Evidence Artifacts, and outputs stay session-scoped
- a decision for one session never unblocks another session
- throughput and latency are recorded for the selected concurrency level
- no duplicate execution occurs when approvals resolve near-simultaneously
- the final report states the tested concurrency level and any observed bottleneck

title: Experiment: approval-backlog-soak escalation and queue pressure
body:
## What
Create `approval-backlog-soak` to measure queueing, escalation, and degradation under sustained
pending work.

## Scenario
- generate a large backlog of pending approvals across several sessions or tenants
- hold some items long enough to trigger escalation and expiry paths
- run long enough to observe scheduler behavior, queue depth, and any degradation trend

## Acceptance
- escalation and expiry happen independently per approval
- queue depth and pending age are recorded over time
- no duplicate escalation events are emitted
- runtime memory, error rate, and backlog metrics are captured for the soak window
- the report distinguishes product defects from environment or host limitations

title: Experiment: micro-saas-toolchain-redo with content-machine and demo-machine realism
body:
## What
Create `micro-saas-toolchain-redo` as a realism-focused rerun of the micro-SaaS experiment.

## Scenario
- keep Portarium as the governor and keep external publish or send effects stubbed for safety
- use `content-machine` where it is actually runnable
- make `demo-machine` runnable or record an explicit skip with reason
- keep the entire run reproducible from tracked config and preflight logs

## Acceptance
- toolchain preflight fails early and clearly when a required tool is unavailable
- the run records which tools were runnable, stubbed, or skipped
- tool usage is captured as evidence rather than inferred only from outputs
- the report states whether the post-validation demo path is proven or still unproven

title: Report: iteration 2 experiment synthesis and residual risk review
body:
## What
Write the iteration 2 comparative scientific report after the reruns and scale experiments complete.

## Include
- direct comparison with iteration 1 results
- what improved, what regressed, and what remained inconclusive
- queue and timing metrics across the suite
- newly found flaws, fixed flaws, and residual risk
- recommendation on whether the governed agent stack is ready for a more production-like business pilot

## Acceptance
- every completed iteration 2 run is referenced with its Evidence Artifacts
- the report separates confirmed behaviour from unproven assumptions
- open follow-up work is converted into specific Beads rather than buried in prose

title: Epic: Cockpit mission control convergence and agent-first operator surface
body:
## Goal
Evolve Cockpit + Portarium from an experiment console into an agent-first operator operating system.

## Product direction
- Cockpit becomes the place operators observe and steer the entire governed system
- Portarium remains the control plane for approvals, policy, evidence, queueing, and recovery
- OpenClaw and other agent runtimes remain the execution layer
- humans provide intent, taste, and insight; agents perform the operational work
- mission-control-ui concepts are absorbed where they improve observability and operator clarity
- plugin-style UI extension points allow new operator surfaces without forking Cockpit

title: ADR: Cockpit and Portarium plugin-extensibility architecture
body:
## What
Write the architecture decision record for Cockpit and Portarium operator-surface extensibility.

## Cover
- how mission-control-ui concepts fold into Cockpit without splitting the operator experience
- how ADR-024 plugin-friendly portal posture becomes a concrete Cockpit and Portarium extension model
- where plugin UI manifests, permissions, data contracts, and action boundaries live
- how agent-generated or agent-selected operator surfaces remain governed rather than arbitrary
- how the control plane stays authoritative while UI extensions remain pluggable

## Acceptance
- decision recorded as a new ADR with options, tradeoffs, and migration path
- references existing mission-control-ui research and integration work
- explicitly states what belongs in Cockpit, Portarium, plugins, and agent runtimes
- defines non-goals so UI extensibility cannot become a governance bypass

title: Spec: operator interaction model for intent, taste, and insight
body:
## What
Define the operator interaction model for an agent-first Portarium system.

## Focus
- humans give intent, taste, and insight
- agents execute the detailed work
- Cockpit surfaces where humans should review, guide, approve, deny, or request changes
- the system distinguishes approval, steering, annotation, and preference signals
- operator input is durable, attributable, and available to the governed run as evidence or context

## Acceptance
- a spec or design note defines the canonical operator inputs and where they attach to Runs, Approvals, Plans, and Evidence Artifacts
- the model distinguishes operator steering from direct tool execution
- it defines how taste and insight are captured without turning operators into micromanagers
- it names the key UI states Cockpit must support for this model

title: Cockpit: mission-control-ui shell and theme convergence
body:
## What
Carry forward the mission-control-ui shell, theme, and operational-awareness patterns into Cockpit.

## Scope
- optional Mission Control theme and design primitives
- Mission Control route or shell integration using the existing cockpit integration research
- status-dense operator layout that improves visibility without replacing core governance surfaces
- reuse or extraction of shared MCU primitives where justified

## Acceptance
- cockpit has a non-breaking Mission Control visual mode or route
- mission-control-ui concepts are mapped to existing Portarium vocabulary and data types
- the implementation does not fork the product into two competing operator consoles
- the resulting surface supports approvals, evidence, and run status with Portarium-native data

title: Cockpit: unified all-claws observability board
body:
## What
Build the unified Cockpit observability surface for all active agent sessions and governed work.

## Scope
- active agent sessions, approvals, pending queue, evidence integrity, tool activity, and run state in one operator surface
- cross-linking between session, approval, run, evidence, and artifact views
- visibility into multiple concurrent agents or "all claws" without cross-session confusion
- timing and queue metrics needed for business-scale operations

## Acceptance
- an operator can identify what every active governed session is doing from Cockpit
- approval waits, stuck sessions, resume events, and backlog are visible without reading raw logs
- a decision or filter in Cockpit never mixes evidence across sessions or workspaces
- the view is backed by Portarium-native APIs or event streams, not ad hoc local scripts

title: Cockpit: launch and steering surfaces for governed agent runs
body:
## What
Add Cockpit surfaces for launching and steering governed agent work.

## Scope
- trigger a new governed run from Cockpit from a human intent statement
- pause, resume, stop, or re-route governed work where policy allows
- attach operator annotations, taste notes, or decision rationale to the run
- hand work off between operators without losing context

## Acceptance
- a human can start and steer a governed run from Cockpit without dropping to scripts for normal operator actions
- actions are policy-gated and recorded as Evidence Artifacts where appropriate
- Cockpit distinguishes launch and steering from approval decisions
- operator handoff leaves a clear durable trail in the run history

title: Platform: Cockpit and Portarium operator-plugin host
body:
## What
Implement the Cockpit and Portarium plugin host for operator-facing UI extensions.

## Scope
- plugin manifest and registration model
- allowed mount points or routes inside Cockpit
- backend contract for plugin data queries and governed commands
- permission model so plugins can read context and request actions safely
- isolation rules so plugin UI can extend the operator console without weakening governance

## Acceptance
- at least one typed plugin contract exists for Cockpit operator extensions
- plugins cannot bypass Portarium policy, approval, or evidence rules
- plugin lifecycle, permissions, and failure behavior are documented and testable
- the host can load a non-core operator surface without changing Cockpit core routes directly

title: Platform: governed agent-generated operator cards and forms
body:
## What
Add the governed path for agent-supplied operator UI contributions.

## Scope
- agent proposes a temporary or contextual operator card, form, or panel when standard Cockpit UI is insufficient
- the proposal is represented as structured UI data, not arbitrary executable code
- Cockpit can render approved operator surfaces inside the plugin host or approved extension frame
- operator interactions with that surface feed back into the governed run as intent, taste, or insight

## Acceptance
- agent-supplied UI contributions use a typed schema and are attributable to a run or approval context
- no arbitrary code execution is required to render the surface
- the approval and evidence model records when a generated surface was proposed, approved, rendered, and used
- the mechanism is suitable for operator-specific workflows without making Cockpit a prompt-generated mess

title: Epic: pilot readiness, policy management, and operator trust
body:
## Goal
Close the remaining gap between technical-feasibility experiments and a real operator pilot.

## Remaining gaps
- prove usefulness against a baseline instead of demo-only success
- prove recovery under crash, restart, redeploy, and provider failure
- let permissioned operators manage Policy and Execution Tier changes from Cockpit
- measure operator trust, approval fatigue, and calibration quality
- govern Cockpit and Portarium plugins plus agent-generated operator surfaces safely
- define telemetry, Evidence Artifact, privacy, retention, and legal-hold rules for all-claws observability
- prepare operator onboarding and a controlled real-world pilot

## Acceptance
- the missing gaps are represented as explicit dependent Beads rather than buried in notes
- the sequence leads to a real go/no-go pilot decision with evidence
- closure means the program has a concrete path from experiment stack to controlled operator use

title: Spec: usefulness scorecard and baseline comparison for governed operator pilots
body:
## What
Define the scorecard that will decide whether Portarium is actually useful in practice.

## Include
- operator minutes per Run
- approval latency and blocked duration
- throughput per operator and per Workspace
- denial, rework, and duplicate-execution rate
- unsafe-action or policy-violation escape rate
- cost per useful outcome including model, tool, and operator cost
- business KPI deltas for the selected workflow

## Acceptance
- a pre-pilot baseline capture plan exists for the chosen business workflow
- the same metric names apply to Growth Studio, micro-SaaS, and the first real pilot
- thresholds for success, concern, and failure are explicit
- the final pilot report can say whether the governed stack beat the current way of working

title: Experiment: operator trust calibration and approval-fatigue study
body:
## What
Run a focused study on whether the approval model creates trust or fatigue.

## Scenario
- operators triage a mixed queue of low-risk and high-risk approvals
- queue pressure lasts long enough to surface fatigue and shortcut behaviour
- sampled review quality is checked against the actual Plan and Evidence Artifact context

## Measure
- time to decision
- approve/deny/request-changes mix
- override rate
- confidence and confusion points
- evidence actually consulted before a decision
- signs of rubber-stamping or unnecessary friction

## Acceptance
- the study identifies where approvals are too opaque, too frequent, or not informative enough
- autonomy-calibration recommendations are converted into concrete Policy or UX follow-up Beads
- the result distinguishes operator training problems from product design problems

title: Experiment: recovery reality for pending approvals and governed resumes
body:
## What
Test the real recovery cases that businesses will hit once governed Runs wait for hours or days.

## Scenario
- an Approval Gate is pending while the agent or control plane is interrupted
- simulate process crash, service restart, deploy, and provider outage variants
- resume after recovery and verify exact-once continuation

## Acceptance
- Approval, Plan, and Evidence Artifact state survives the interruption
- Cockpit surfaces the degraded or waiting state clearly to operators
- resume does not replay prior writes or lose operator decisions
- the result bundle records which failures were product defects versus host or environment limitations

title: Cockpit: permissioned policy authoring and simulation surface
body:
## What
Add the real operator surface for managing Policy and Execution Tier behaviour from Cockpit.

## Focus
- permissioned operators can change an action class from Auto to HumanApprove, or to policy-blocked, without code edits
- show blast radius, scope, and impacted Workspaces or Runs before applying the change
- simulate how pending and future approvals would behave under the new rule
- build on the earlier policy-editor showcase work rather than creating another disconnected mock

## Acceptance
- a permissioned operator can change a governed action policy from Cockpit
- the UI shows the current rule, proposed diff, rationale, and expected impact
- risky changes are visibly different from low-risk changes
- the surface uses Portarium-native data and semantics rather than demo-only fixtures

title: Governance: versioned policy change workflow with scope, expiry, approval, and rollback
body:
## What
Make policy changes themselves governed, versioned, attributable, and reversible.

## Include
- scoped changes by Workspace, action class, or broader tenancy boundary
- maker-checker / Separation of Duties for high-risk policy edits
- effective-from and expiry windows
- rationale capture and diff preview
- rollback and supersede behaviour
- explicit semantics for active Runs versus future Runs

## Acceptance
- policy changes produce a durable audit trail with who, why, when, and scope
- high-risk policy edits can require approval before they become effective
- rollback is a first-class operation, not an ad hoc manual repair
- operators can answer why an action was allowed, blocked, or HumanApprove at any point in time

title: Security: operator-plugin governance controls for Cockpit and Portarium
body:
## What
Define and implement the governance controls required before Cockpit and Portarium plugins can be trusted.

## Include
- plugin identity, signing or attestation, and version pinning
- permission grants for reads, commands, and governed actions
- kill switch and emergency disable flow
- failure isolation and rollback behaviour
- audit trail for plugin install, enable, disable, and upgrade

## Acceptance
- plugins cannot become a governance bypass or hidden privileged path
- operators can see what a plugin is allowed to do before enabling it
- emergency disable is testable and fast
- the controls are strong enough for business-facing operator surfaces, not just local demos

title: Governance: data lifecycle for all-claws telemetry, evidence, privacy, and legal hold
body:
## What
Define how the new all-claws observability and trace surfaces handle data over time.

## Cover
- what agent transcripts, tool traces, screenshots, timelines, and Evidence Artifacts are retained
- redaction and privacy boundaries for operator-visible telemetry
- retention classes, deletion rules, and legal-hold interaction
- visibility boundaries by role, Workspace, and tenant
- how experimental trace depth maps to production-safe defaults

## Acceptance
- the operator console can expose deep observability without creating uncontrolled data sprawl
- retention and privacy rules are explicit for new experiment and pilot telemetry
- legal hold and evidentiary integrity are preserved where required
- the result updates the path for Cockpit observability, not just experiment folders

title: Security: threat model and hardening tests for agent-generated operator surfaces
body:
## What
Pressure-test the safety model for agent-supplied operator cards, forms, and panels.

## Threats
- arbitrary code or script execution through generated surfaces
- prompt-injection or content-driven unsafe actions
- hidden privileged commands in a typed UI payload
- misleading or manipulative operator UX
- cross-run or cross-tenant context leakage

## Acceptance
- a documented threat model exists for agent-generated operator UI
- negative tests and contract tests cover the main abuse paths
- rendering stays schema-driven and non-executable by default
- the feature is blocked from broader rollout until the hardening evidence is acceptable

title: Enablement: operator onboarding and incident playbooks for Cockpit-driven operations
body:
## What
Prepare the human side of the system so it is usable by more than the original builder.

## Include
- onboarding path for first-time operators
- normal-run playbooks for approve, deny, request-changes, handoff, and escalation
- incident playbooks for stuck Runs, broken resumes, plugin disable, and policy rollback
- guidance on when to trust the system versus inspect deeply

## Acceptance
- a new operator can run a governed workflow from Cockpit with minimal handholding
- common failure and recovery modes have an executable playbook
- the material supports the first controlled pilot rather than a lab-only demo

title: Pilot: first controlled operator-team workflow on the governed stack
body:
## What
Run the first real, narrow pilot with a human operator team using Cockpit and Portarium for normal work.

## Shape
- choose one recurring workflow with bounded risk
- use real operators for days or weeks, not minutes
- compare against the pre-pilot baseline
- keep high-liability external actions governed appropriately

## Acceptance
- at least one real operator team uses Cockpit as the normal operating surface for the pilot workflow
- the pilot records throughput, latency, operator effort, trust signals, cost, and business outcome
- serious governance failures or duplicate actions are explicitly called out, not averaged away
- the result is strong enough to say whether the stack is genuinely useful or still lab-only

title: Report: pilot readiness gate and real-world usefulness decision
body:
## What
Write the decision report after the first controlled pilot completes.

## Include
- comparison to the baseline scorecard
- what improved, what regressed, and what remained inconclusive
- operator trust and fatigue findings
- recovery and policy-management readiness
- plugin and agent-generated UI residual risk
- recommendation on whether Portarium is ready for broader business use

## Acceptance
- the report makes a clear go / no-go / continue-contained-pilot recommendation
- conclusions are backed by experiment and pilot Evidence Artifacts
- remaining gaps are turned into concrete Beads rather than vague future work

title: Governance: policy shadow evaluation and historical replay before activation
body:
## What
Add a safe pre-activation path for Policy changes so operators can see their real impact before rollout.

## Include
- replay a candidate policy version against recent governed Runs, Approvals, and queued work without mutating live state
- show counterfactual changes in tier selection, approval volume, blocked actions, expiry risk, and cost impact
- compare current policy versus proposed policy using the same metric names as the iteration-2 telemetry pack
- attach the replay report to the governed policy-change record as evidence

## Acceptance
- a permissioned operator can review how a proposed policy change would have affected recent governed work
- the replay path is read-only and cannot trigger live actions or mutate approval state
- risky policy edits can require a replay report before activation
- the result is suitable for Cockpit policy simulation, not only ad hoc scripts

title: Experiment: shift-aware approval routing, delegation, and after-hours coverage
body:
## What
Create an experiment that tests how governed work behaves when the primary operator is unavailable and coverage must move across people or time windows.

## Scenario
- a governed Run creates pending approvals outside the primary operator's active window
- delegation or coverage rules route approvals to another eligible operator or queue
- at least one approval waits across a shift boundary and another is escalated after missing the coverage window
- the agent resumes correctly once a covered operator decides

## Acceptance
- assignment changes, delegation windows, and escalations are recorded as evidence
- an unavailable or sleeping operator does not silently stall all governed work forever
- Separation of Duties and eligibility rules still apply after delegation or shift handoff
- the report compares after-hours coverage behaviour to the existing operator-team handoff experiment

title: Governance: workspace and run autonomy budgets with hard-stop controls
body:
## What
Define and enforce generic autonomy budgets so agents cannot run indefinitely or spend freely even when approvals are technically valid.

## Include
- workspace-level and run-level caps for model spend, tool-call count, outbound actions, and approval volume
- soft warning thresholds versus hard-stop thresholds
- freeze or kill-switch semantics for budget exhaustion and runaway behaviour
- evidence and operator-visible rationale when a budget cap pauses or blocks governed work

## Acceptance
- autonomy budgets are first-class Policy inputs rather than experiment-only conventions
- operators can tell whether a Run stopped because of policy, budget, or infrastructure failure
- hard-stop behaviour is deterministic and auditable
- the pilot path includes budget and stop-loss controls before real business use

title: Governance: outbound communication compliance for consent, suppression, quiet hours, and jurisdictional send rules
body:
## What
Connect governed outbound actions to the real communication-compliance checks a business needs before email, messaging, or publishing can be trusted.

## Include
- contact consent and suppression checks before send or publish actions
- quiet-hour or business-window rules by recipient or workspace timezone
- jurisdiction or channel-specific constraints that can force HumanApprove or ManualOnly
- clear operator-visible rationale when an outbound action is blocked, escalated, or deferred for compliance reasons

## Acceptance
- outbound governance is not limited to blast radius and approvals; compliance state affects execution too
- experiments and pilots can use deterministic fake compliance fixtures instead of skipping the problem
- evidence records why an outbound action was allowed, deferred, or blocked
- the path is suitable for Growth Studio and micro-saas workflows where sending is a real business action

title: Cockpit: approver coverage, delegation, and roster management
body:
## What
Add the operator surface for managing who is expected to handle governed work across teams and time windows.

## Scope
- configure approver coverage windows, delegates, specialist routing, and fallback queues
- preview which operator or group would receive a given approval class under the current rules
- make coverage changes attributable, permissioned, and visible in Cockpit
- show when coverage state, not policy alone, is the reason work is waiting or rerouted

## Acceptance
- a permissioned operator can manage approval coverage without editing code or hidden config
- Cockpit can explain why a pending approval is assigned, delegated, waiting for coverage, or escalated
- coverage changes leave a durable audit trail suitable for later incident review
- the surface supports the operator-team and after-hours experiments rather than staying as a one-off admin screen

title: Governance: delegated autonomy hierarchy and precedence rules
body:
## What
Turn the operator interaction model into concrete configuration semantics across platform baseline, tenant, Workspace, role or queue, Run charter, and Action scopes.

## Include
- precedence and inheritance rules across each configuration layer
- explicit distinction between tightening a rule and weakening a rule
- approved override and break-glass path for exceptional weakening where allowed
- non-overridable platform invariants that no tenant setting can bypass
- effective-rule explanation suitable for Cockpit and API responses

## Acceptance
- the hierarchy of autonomy control is documented with deterministic precedence rules
- lower scopes cannot silently weaken higher-scope hard limits
- operators can explain why an effective tier, budget, or prohibition applied to a given Action
- the result is usable by policy authoring, simulation, and audit flows rather than living as prose only

title: Governance: authority and accountability model for human oversight functions
body:
## What
Define the responsibility, authority, and accountability model for the human functions in a Portarium deployment.

## Include
- mapping from governance functions to current roles: admin, operator, approver, auditor
- future-safe handling for policy owner and domain SME responsibilities without collapsing everything into admin
- which interventions each function may perform: steer, approve, deny, reroute, handoff, override, audit, policy change
- separation-of-duties rules for risky policy edits and irreversible Actions
- accountable-decision trail requirements for every non-routine intervention

## Acceptance
- the difference between operator, approver, auditor, policy owner, and domain SME is explicit
- every intervention type has a named accountable actor and authority source
- small-team overlap is allowed but not assumed to be safe by default
- the result can drive RBAC refinement, Cockpit permissions, and audit review without ambiguity

title: Contract: epistemic access and evidence sufficiency packet for human decisions
body:
## What
Define the minimum context packet and evidence sufficiency contract required before a human can steer, approve, deny, or override governed work.

## Include
- declared goal, scope boundary, current step, and next-step preview
- proposed Action or policy change with blast radius and reversibility label
- policy rationale, Execution Tier, budget impact, and compliance implications
- uncertainty, anomaly, and missing-evidence signalling
- provenance for upstream agent-generated inputs that materially affect the current decision
- explicit request-more-evidence path instead of forcing binary approve or deny when context is insufficient

## Acceptance
- a shared contract or schema exists for decision context packets
- approval, steering, and policy-change surfaces can consume the same packet shape
- operators no longer need raw-log archaeology for routine high-signal decisions
- missing evidence is visible as a first-class state, not hidden behind fluent summaries

title: Cockpit: intervention control surface for steering, freeze, handoff, and emergency actions
body:
## What
Define and implement the Cockpit control surface for real human intervention over governed Runs.

## Include
- pause, resume, reroute, handoff, escalate, request-more-evidence, freeze, sandbox, and emergency disable semantics
- clear separation between monitoring, steering, approval, and policy-change actions in the UI
- state handling for waiting, blocked, degraded, frozen, and operator-owned work
- evidence-backed recording for every non-routine intervention
- guardrails so generic buttons cannot disguise materially different consequences or authority levels

## Acceptance
- Cockpit exposes meaningful intervention actions beyond approve or deny
- every intervention is policy-gated, attributable, and visible in run history
- operators can tell whether they are steering work, deciding an Approval Gate, or changing policy
- the surface supports calm supervision without hiding emergency or break-glass paths

title: Enablement: operator readiness, AI literacy, and calibration model for governed autonomy
body:
## What
Define the readiness model for who is allowed to steer or approve high-impact governed work in a near-autonomous Portarium deployment.

## Include
- required domain competence and AI literacy for different authority levels
- calibration guidance for appropriate reliance rather than blanket trust or blanket skepticism
- workload and fatigue limits that can invalidate nominal oversight in practice
- verification sampling or similar anti-skill-atrophy mechanisms
- how readiness differs for operator, approver, auditor, policy owner, and domain SME functions

## Acceptance
- organisations can define eligibility requirements beyond static RBAC labels
- the model distinguishes authority from readiness and training status
- pilot readiness can answer whether the human side of oversight is actually prepared, not just present on paper
- the result feeds operator onboarding, trust calibration, and approval-fatigue work instead of duplicating them

title: Contract: approval feedback reason taxonomy and routing semantics
body:
## What
Define the structured rejection, revision, and feedback model for governed approvals and steering actions.

## Include
- a typed reason taxonomy for deny, request-changes, lower-scope, and escalation decisions
- distinction between feedback that applies only to the current Run and feedback that should update reusable workflow or policy assets
- capture of whether the issue was goal selection, evidence quality, risk classification, execution plan, missing context, policy violation, or substantive quality failure
- linkage from feedback to approval records, Evidence Artifacts, and future calibration surfaces
- Cockpit form requirements so feedback stays fast but actionable

## Acceptance
- approval outcomes are not limited to pass or fail with an unstructured note
- feedback can be routed to the current Run, reusable playbook, prompt strategy, Policy, or operator enablement path
- audit review can explain not only what was denied, but why and what changed as a result
- the model supports product learning without turning every denial into ad hoc prose archaeology

title: Cockpit: risk-tiered approval card contract and triage-to-deep-review escalation
body:
## What
Define the approval-card information architecture and review-depth escalation rules for Cockpit under the agent-first operator model.

## Include
- the minimum fields every approval or steering card must show: proposed Action, goal or intent, systems touched, Policy result, blast radius, reversibility, evidence, rationale, and prior related Actions
- the conditions that keep a case in fast triage versus escalate it into deep review
- friction rules for high-risk or irreversible Actions: required expansion, rationale, second-step confirm, or escalation lock
- mobile triage posture versus desktop control-room posture without splitting the underlying governance model
- consistency with the older swipe-triage and review-mode work while adapting it to current Portarium-native semantics

## Acceptance
- Cockpit has a named contract for card content and review-depth escalation
- low-risk triage stays fast without hiding material risk or evidence
- high-risk review becomes visibly deeper and harder to rubber-stamp
- the design can drive implementation and QA for both mobile and desktop approval surfaces

title: Cockpit: policy overview dashboard for posture, drift, and ownership
body:
## What
Build the operational landing surface for Cockpit policy management so policy owners start from posture, drift, and incidents rather than from a raw rule table.

## Include
- posture summary by Execution Tier and major capability families
- top risky capabilities and recent denials or escalations
- noisy approval classes and drift signals
- recent overrides and break-glass usage
- visible ownership, last-review state, and linked incidents or near misses by policy area
- entry points into capability posture, rule editing, and simulation flows

## Acceptance
- policy management starts from operational posture rather than syntax
- humans can identify where Policy is too weak, too noisy, or unowned
- the surface links runtime incidents and approval noise back to policy objects
- the result complements, rather than duplicates, detailed rule editing surfaces

title: Cockpit: capability posture matrix for default tiers, roles, and evidence requirements
body:
## What
Implement the primary policy-setting view as a capability posture matrix rather than forcing bespoke rules for every case.

## Include
- grouped capability families such as read, write, delete, money, deploy, and admin with drill-down to specific capabilities where needed
- posture controls across environment, resource or system, data sensitivity, blast radius, persistence, Execution Tier, allowed roles, and required evidence
- visible inheritance from higher policy layers and clear indication of exception rules
- presets and progressive disclosure so the matrix does not become an unreadable grid dump
- natural-language explanation of the effective posture for a selected capability slice

## Acceptance
- policy owners can set default autonomy doctrine through the matrix without editing raw rules
- the matrix answers who, what, where, sensitivity, blast radius, evidence, and tier clearly for each posture slice
- lower-level exceptions are visible against the default posture instead of being hidden in separate rules
- the surface is suitable for real Cockpit policy authoring, not just a design artifact

title: Cockpit: approval-to-policy conversion workflow from runtime precedent
body:
## What
Add the runtime-to-governance loop that lets operators and approvers convert concrete approval decisions into reusable Policy changes.

## Include
- decision actions such as deny once, deny and create rule, approve once, approve and loosen rule, require more evidence next time, and escalate this class of Action
- prefilled conversion from the current approval context into suggested capability, environment, evidence, role, or tier changes
- explicit distinction between one-off Run decisions and reusable Policy mutations
- linkage from the resulting Policy change back to the motivating approval, incident, or near miss
- compatibility with structured feedback reasons and policy simulation before activation

## Acceptance
- Cockpit can turn runtime judgment into reusable governance without forcing manual re-entry in a separate screen
- the approval-to-policy path preserves rationale, context, and auditability
- users can choose whether feedback affects only this Run or future similar cases
- the workflow makes precedent one of the main ways Policy improves over time

title: Cockpit: layered policy studio prototype and operator showcase
body:
## What
Create a mock-first Cockpit prototype for layered policy authoring and governed autonomy review, anchored in the existing approvals and OpenClaw demo fixtures.

## Include
- prototype the policy overview, capability posture matrix, simulation/replay lab, and approval-to-policy handoff inside Cockpit
- identify and implement the minimum route, component, fixture, and navigation changes needed to review the UX in-app
- preserve the fast approvals/Tinder triage posture while connecting it to policy authoring and control-room depth
- prepare a showcase-ready slice that can be reviewed before turning it into a polished demo-machine storyline

## Acceptance
- a reviewer can open Cockpit locally and walk the proposed policy-authoring UX without relying on prose alone
- the prototype makes the runtime-to-policy loop visible rather than leaving policy as a detached editor
- the change list for production Cockpit and the follow-on demo-machine work is explicit
- the slice is testable and ready for a later showcase capture pass

title: HCI: mental-model audit for Policy, authority, and Execution Tier understanding in Cockpit
body:
## What
Run a focused mental-model audit on how operators understand Policy, authority, capability posture, and Execution Tier concepts while exploring Cockpit.

## Review
- whether operators can tell the difference between capability, rule, approval route, required evidence, role gate, and effective Policy
- where current labels or groupings create concept collisions across Policy Studio and approvals
- which questions operators naturally ask first when exploring a tool, connector, or governed Action
- what belongs in overview, drill-down, simulation, and approval-card contexts instead of being shown everywhere at once

## Acceptance
- the result identifies the main concept mismatches between system semantics and likely operator expectations
- findings are tied to concrete exploratory tasks rather than generic UX opinion
- terminology and information-architecture gaps are translated into follow-on design or implementation work
- the audit makes it easier to explain why a given Action is Auto, Assisted, Human-approve, or Manual-only

title: HCI: cognitive-friction review of policy exploration and approval-to-policy workflows
body:
## What
Evaluate where the current Cockpit policy and authority flows create avoidable cognitive load, hidden state, or navigation churn.

## Review
- memory load while moving between overview, posture matrix, rule detail, simulation, and live approval contexts
- ambiguity around what will happen now versus what will change for future governed work
- places where evidence, blast radius, scope, or authority are visible too late
- whether the handoff between Policy Studio and approvals encourages comprehension or just fast clicking
- where the interface is noisy because everything is surfaced at once instead of progressively disclosed

## Acceptance
- the review produces a prioritized friction log with task context and likely operator impact
- findings distinguish copy problems, information-architecture problems, and control-model problems
- the result identifies where the current UI risks shallow confidence, rubber-stamping, or exploratory confusion
- recommendations are concrete enough to drive the next prototype or usability pass

title: Spec: operator-facing terminology and progressive-disclosure model for policy authoring
body:
## What
Define the operator-facing conceptual model for how Cockpit explains Policy, authority, evidence, and Execution Tier decisions without overwhelming the user.

## Include
- a canonical vocabulary for operator-facing labels across Policy Studio, approvals, simulation, and audit views
- summary-versus-detail rules so each surface answers the first-order questions before showing deeper mechanics
- explanation patterns for effective Policy: who can act, on what, where, under which conditions, with what evidence, and why human review is required
- disclosure rules for advanced concepts such as inheritance, overrides, break-glass, blast radius, and historical replay
- examples of how tool, connector, and capability semantics should be narrated in plain language without losing Portarium precision

## Acceptance
- Cockpit has a named operator-facing explanation model rather than ad hoc copy per screen
- policy and authority surfaces answer the common operator questions in a predictable order
- advanced governance detail is available without being the default reading burden for exploration
- the output is specific enough to guide a clarity-focused UI pass on the existing prototype

title: Cockpit: preserve Policy Studio draft state across policy-to-approval round trips
body: ## What

title: Cockpit: explicit working-context and time-horizon cues for policy authoring
body:
## What
Add explicit working-context and time-horizon cues to Policy Studio so operators can tell what live case they are looking at, what draft Policy they are editing, and what changes now versus later.

## Include
- a clear live-case summary tied to the Approval that motivated the draft
- explicit distinction between current approval work and future Policy effect
- plainer operator-facing labels for summary tiles and context sections that currently depend on insider Portarium vocabulary
- clearer separation between published posture, draft changes, and required evidence
- preservation of the current round-trip draft restoration behavior

## Acceptance
- Policy Studio answers what case triggered this draft, what Policy is being edited, and what future impact is being proposed without requiring prior training
- summary cards and section labels reduce jargon and improve scanability
- the active draft state is visually distinct from published Policy defaults
- route tests cover the new context and time-horizon cues
- the change aligns with cockpit-policy-clarity-pass-v1.md rather than adding another disconnected concept layer

title: Cockpit: focused policy-linked approval review mode
body:
## What
Add a focused approval review mode for Approvals opened from Policy Studio so the operator sees the immediate decision path before dense governance metadata.

## Include
- a visible policy-linked review state when entering the Approvals route from Policy Studio
- above-the-fold emphasis on proposed Action, why the case is gated, recommendation, and primary decision controls
- clearer separation between decide-now content, evidence sufficiency, and deeper audit detail
- strong return navigation back to the preserved Policy Studio draft
- compatibility with the existing triage deck and generic Approvals flow

## Acceptance
- operators can tell why the current Approval is in front of them and what decision is required within the first viewport
- the primary decision controls are easier to find when entering from Policy Studio
- Policy, evidence, and audit metadata remain available without burying the main task
- policy-linked review keeps the return path and draft restoration intact
- route or component tests cover focused review cues introduced by the change

title: Cockpit: heuristic-driven clarity pass for policy authoring and approval review
body:
## What
Turn the April 3 heuristic review into a concrete Cockpit clarity pass across Policy Studio and the policy-linked approval review flow.

## Scope
- reduce concept density in Policy Studio so the operator can tell what they are editing now, what changes later, and what concrete case motivated the draft
- add explicit working-context and time-horizon cues for policy authoring so the page answers current case vs future Policy without requiring prior Portarium training
- make the approval review mode more focused when entered from Policy Studio, with the primary decision path visible before deep governance metadata
- preserve the existing policy-to-approval round-trip and draft restoration flow while making the operator mental model easier to acquire
- update specs and backlog references so the clarity pass is reviewable and reversible

## Acceptance
- Policy Studio establishes a clear primary task and distinguishes current live case, draft Policy, and future impact at a glance
- policy-linked approval review surfaces why the case is in front of the operator, what decision is needed now, and where to return afterward
- terminology and hierarchy reduce reliance on internal Portarium jargon such as slice or posture where plain-language alternatives are possible
- route tests cover the new clarity cues and policy-linked review behavior
- the change is validated with screenshots and a follow-up heuristic pass before closure

title: Governance: run charter and delegated authority contract for bounded autonomous Runs
body:
## What
Define the Run charter that lets a human delegate meaningful authority up front so normal work stops waiting for per-Action approvals.

## Include
- a typed charter object attached to every governed Run with goal, success condition, scope boundary, allowed Action classes, blocked classes, budget caps, time window, evidence depth, and escalation triggers
- explicit distinction between what the agent may decide locally versus what must surface as an Approval Gate or intervention request
- inheritance from platform, tenant, Workspace, and role policy layers without allowing silent weakening of harder limits
- charter diff and approval flow when a running Run needs more authority than originally delegated
- a Cockpit summary that makes the active charter and delegated authority legible to operators and approvers

## Acceptance
- a governed Run can execute a bounded set of normal Actions without creating redundant Approval Gates
- Actions outside charter scope or authority deterministically escalate rather than guessing
- the active charter is attributable, auditable, and visible alongside Run and Approval context
- charter expansion or override leaves a durable evidence trail and does not mutate history in place

title: Governance: exception routing and anomaly-trigger semantics for delegated autonomy
body:
## What
Define the typed exception model that decides when agents continue autonomously and when work must surface to a human.

## Include
- exception classes for uncertainty, novelty, repeated failure, policy edge cases, missing evidence, degraded dependency, irreversible action, blast-radius jump, persistence creation, and compliance risk
- routing rules from each exception class to operator, approver, auditor, or specialist queue
- calm-state handling for expected low-risk exceptions versus alert-state handling for urgent or runaway conditions
- suppression, deduplication, and batching rules so exception handling does not recreate approval spam under a different name
- evidence packet expectations and next-step options for every surfaced exception

## Acceptance
- the control plane can explain why an Action continued autonomously versus why it escalated
- repeated low-signal anomalies do not flood the operator queue with duplicate work
- every surfaced exception names the responsible human function, authority path, and expected next action
- the model is usable by Cockpit triage, control-room review, and policy simulation rather than living as prose only

title: Telemetry: autonomy delegation, exception-rate, and policy-learning scorecard
body:
## What
Make it measurable whether Portarium is moving beyond per-Action approvals toward healthy delegated autonomy.

## Include
- counts and ratios for auto-resolved Actions, exception-routed Actions, human-approved Actions, Manual-only Actions, and emergency stops
- approval-volume trend, repeated-exception hotspots, policy churn, and precedent-to-policy conversion rate
- time-to-decision and time-to-resume broken out by routine approval, exception escalation, and policy change
- unsafe-escape and false-escalation indicators using the same event vocabulary as policy replay and pilot scorecards
- a Cockpit-ready dashboard or export path suitable for longitudinal pilot review

## Acceptance
- an organisation can tell whether approvals are shrinking for the right reasons rather than by hiding risk
- runtime exceptions can be linked to policy improvement, policy regression, or operator-load problems
- pilot reporting can compare governance maturity over time instead of only raw throughput
- the scorecard complements the usefulness baseline rather than duplicating it

title: Governance: verification sampling and post-action audit queue for delegated autonomy
body:
## What
Add the post-action verification layer that preserves human judgment, catches silent failure, and prevents skill atrophy as more work moves out of the live approval queue.

## Include
- sampling rules by Action class, Execution Tier, blast radius, novelty, and operator or agent track record
- a dedicated audit queue for after-the-fact review of completed Actions and Runs
- structured outcomes such as correct, risky-but-allowed, should-have-escalated, policy-too-strict, and evidence-insufficient
- automatic routing from sampled findings into Policy changes, runbook updates, prompt strategy, or operator enablement
- controls to increase sampling during drift, incidents, new capability rollout, or degraded provider posture

## Acceptance
- delegated autonomy can be checked without forcing every Action through a blocking approval
- sampled review findings feed reusable governance changes rather than dying as one-off notes
- Cockpit can show sampling coverage and current confidence by capability or Action class
- pilot readiness can prove that lower approval volume did not simply hide defects

title: Cockpit: true single-case semantics for policy-linked focused review
body:
## What
Finish the policy-linked focused review flow so it behaves as a real single-case review session instead of a cosmetic variant of the general triage queue.

## Include
- lock the focused review session to the requested Approval instead of silently falling through to unrelated queue items
- explicit end-state behavior after approve, deny, request-changes, or skip: return to Policy Studio, close the session, or let the operator choose the next action deliberately
- graceful handling when the targeted Approval is no longer pending, no longer visible, or already decided
- route and component tests covering focused-review action flow, missing-target handling, and queue-isolation behavior
- compatibility with the preserved Policy Studio draft and return path

## Acceptance
- a focused review opened from Policy Studio never advances the operator into an unrelated Approval without an explicit choice
- the UI can explain what happened if the targeted Approval no longer exists or no longer needs review
- focused review semantics are test-covered rather than inferred from general triage behavior
- the result matches the operator promise made by the policy-linked review copy

title: Cockpit: stale-data and sync visibility safeguards for focused approval review
body:
## What
Preserve operational safety signals when Approvals is opened in focused review mode from Policy Studio.

## Include
- stale-data visibility rules for focused review, not just fully offline state or queued outbox writes
- clear distinction between live network data, cached data, and possibly stale approval context when a human is about to decide
- test coverage for focused-review rendering under stale-data, offline, and pending-sync conditions
- copy and layout rules that keep safety banners visible without collapsing the focused-review mental model
- compatibility with the return-to-Policy-Studio flow and single-case review semantics

## Acceptance
- focused review does not hide stale or unsynced state that would matter to an approval decision
- operators can tell when they are deciding on cached versus current approval data
- the safety signaling works in both phone-first focused review and desktop review contexts
- regression tests cover the sync-warning rules introduced by the focused mode

title: Epic: software-first autonomous venture proving ground
body:
## Goal
Keep the near-term Portarium proving path software-first while preserving robotics as a later expansion instead of a current delivery target.

## Intent
Unify the existing Growth Studio, micro-SaaS, content, and operator-governance work into one coherent proving program:
- agents read trusted sources and build useful software or media artifacts
- humans provide objectives, taste, boundary conditions, and approvals where needed
- Cockpit becomes the control surface for multiple governed Projects, not just one demo queue
- self-use proof comes before broader public claims
- robotics remains explicitly out of scope until capital, hardware access, and physical safety testing exist

## Success criteria
- one bounded software workflow is genuinely used by the builder
- a public demo can show multiple governed Project types without pretending all are production-ready
- the backlog clearly separates proving-the-system work from speculative expansion

title: Strategy: choose the first software-first proving workflow and public narrative
body:
## What
Make the strategic choice about which software-first workflow Portarium should prove first instead of treating Growth Studio, content automation, and micro-SaaS building as equal priorities.

## Compare
- source-to-content studio
- source-to-micro-SaaS builder
- software distribution or growth loop
- a hybrid path where one Project creates content while another builds product artifacts

## Acceptance
- one primary proving workflow is chosen for self-use and near-term implementation
- one secondary workflow is named as showcase-only rather than equal priority
- explicit non-goals and deferred tracks are recorded
- success criteria and evidence requirements map back to existing and new Beads

title: Spec: trusted source ingestion, citation, and research-dossier loop for autonomous builders
body:
## What
Define the governed research loop for agents that read external sources and turn them into cited internal artifacts for content or software-building decisions.

## Include
- source classes and trust ranking rules for official docs, repos, public web pages, competitor material, and user-provided references
- freshness expectations and when a source must be re-read before acting
- a research dossier or opportunity brief artifact with citations, confidence, and claim boundaries
- operator-visible provenance from source to dossier to downstream Derived Artifact
- escalation rules when the agent wants to act on weak, stale, conflicting, or uncited material

## Acceptance
- generated claims that affect product or content direction are traceable to source evidence
- uncited or low-trust material cannot silently drive autonomous execution
- the same loop can support content ideation, micro-SaaS opportunity shaping, and later showcase flows

title: Cockpit: Project portfolio and governed venture surface for multiple autonomous Projects
body:
## What
Add the Project-level operator model and Cockpit surface so multiple governed software ventures or campaigns can coexist without collapsing into one global queue.

## Include
- Project as the container for Run charters, taste notes, approvals, Evidence Artifacts, and scorecards
- portfolio or project-switcher views for content Projects, micro-SaaS Projects, and internal experiments
- per-Project policy posture, autonomy budget summary, current health, and active risks
- clear separation of approvals, artifacts, and outcomes by Project so context does not bleed across efforts
- linkage from Projects to external Systems of Record when an experiment becomes a real workflow

## Acceptance
- operators can tell which governed Run belongs to which Project without log archaeology
- multiple Projects can be supervised from Cockpit without mixing context or evidence
- the surface supports the user's 'projects inside' model rather than assuming a single long-running experiment

title: Contract: Project brief, taste signals, and artifact acceptance loop for content and micro-SaaS work
body:
## What
Turn operator intent and taste into a concrete Project-level contract for generated artifacts instead of leaving creative direction as ad hoc comments.

## Include
- a typed Project brief covering objective, audience, constraints, style anchors, quality bar, and non-goals
- operator inputs for ideas, taste notes, critique, and acceptance thresholds that do not require micromanaging each step
- artifact review semantics for content, landing pages, specs, prototypes, and other Derived Artifacts
- reusable accept, reject, request-changes, and 'wrong direction' signals that can influence future artifact generation
- separation between one-off critique for a single artifact and durable Project-level preference updates

## Acceptance
- humans can provide ideas and taste in a structured form that agents can actually use
- acceptance or rejection of content and micro-SaaS artifacts follows a named contract rather than free-form taste notes only
- the same model works across creative and product-like outputs without flattening them into generic approvals

title: Pilot: self-use alpha on one recurring software workflow with evidence and rollback
body:
## What
Run a real self-use alpha on one recurring software-first workflow so the system is proven through actual use before broader public claims.

## Shape
- choose one recurring workflow from the strategy decision rather than trying to prove every direction at once
- use Portarium, Cockpit, and the governed agent stack for real work over days or weeks, not minutes
- record manual fallback, rollback, and stop-using-it moments instead of hiding them
- capture whether the system saves time, creates useful artifacts, and stays within acceptable error or recovery limits

## Acceptance
- the builder can honestly say the workflow is being used for real, not only demoed
- outcome evidence includes operator effort, exception rate, rollback events, failures, and useful outputs
- the alpha identifies whether the current stack is merely impressive or actually trustworthy for one bounded workflow
- follow-up Beads can be grounded in lived usage rather than imagined operator behaviour

title: Experiment: cited source-to-artifact loop for content and micro-SaaS candidates
body:
## What
Create a software-first experiment where agents read trusted sources, assemble a research dossier, and then produce governed artifacts for either content or a micro-SaaS candidate.

## Scenario
- ingest a bounded set of trusted sources around a problem or niche
- generate a cited research dossier and opportunity brief
- produce one or more downstream artifacts such as content packs, landing-page copy, product specs, or prototype briefs
- request human taste feedback, scope correction, or approval where the artifact crosses quality or risk gates
- record the full evidence path from source material to final artifact decision

## Acceptance
- the same experiment path can produce both media-style and product-style outputs from the same source base
- generated artifacts carry forward citation and confidence context instead of becoming detached drafts
- operators can intervene on goal, taste, scope, or evidence quality without collapsing the whole Run into manual work
- the result is suitable for a public demo and a later self-use alpha, not just a lab-only script

title: Demo-machine: multi-Project software-first autonomy showcase
body:
## What
Turn the current single-flow showcase into a broader demo-machine narrative that can show how Portarium governs multiple software-first Projects from phone and dashboard surfaces.

## Include
- one content-oriented Project and one micro-SaaS or product-oriented Project with distinct briefs, artifacts, and policy posture
- mobile approval or exception handling flow plus desktop Cockpit views for Project switching, Policy, and evidence
- visible Run charter, policy posture, evidence chain, and intervention controls for OpenClaw-driven work
- a storyline that shows humans providing ideas, taste, and boundaries while agents execute the detailed work
- a recording-ready flow that is compelling without pretending every underlying path is already production-grade

## Acceptance
- the showcase can demonstrate governed autonomy across more than one Project type without confusing the audience
- the demo clearly shows why Cockpit is more than a swipe UI and why Portarium is more than a toy agent wrapper
- the path is concrete enough to support public attention work after the self-use alpha starts producing real proof
